claims.claim_text: The opening logistics-company overrun anecdote presents a real 2023 event with named timing and exact cost figures.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: In 2023 a logistics company presented its board with a plan to migrate from AWS to on-premises Kubernetes. The infrastructure team built the case on a single number: hardware. They priced servers, switches, and storage at three hundred and eighty thousand dollars, walked the CFO through the spreadsheet, and got approval the same week. Twelve months later the actual run rate was one point one million dollars and rising. They had not budgeted for colocation at seventy two thousand a year, redundant internet circuits at thirty six thousand, a part-time facilities manager at forty five thousand, two extra engineers at three hundred and fifty thousand combined, hardware support contracts at thirty eight thousand, or one hundred and eighty thousand in professional services to wire the first racks. The "savings" against cloud disappeared completely, and the team spent a quarter explaining the variance instead of shipping product.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams regularly underestimate on-prem migrations when they budget only the hardware line and omit recurring facility, staffing, support, and deployment costs. When those missing categories are added later, the expected cloud savings can disappear and the project can spend months explaining variance instead of shipping product.
claims.rationale: This is a war story with fake-precise numbers and no attributable primary source.

claims.claim_text: The non-hardware portion of on-prem TCO is typically three to five times the hardware cost over three years.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | third paragraph
claims.anchor_text: The submerged portion is typically three to five times the hardware cost over a three year horizon, and the ratio gets worse at smaller scale because fixed costs do not amortise across a tiny fleet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: At small and mid-sized scale, non-hardware costs can outweigh the hardware line by a wide margin because fixed costs do not amortise well across a small fleet.
claims.rationale: The exact ratio is a load-bearing statistic but no primary benchmark is provided.

claims.claim_text: Salary is roughly two thirds of total engineer cost, and a fully loaded multiplier of 1.3 to 1.5 should be applied to base salary.
claims.span_hint: section: Operating Expenditure (OpEx) | paragraph after OPEX table
claims.anchor_text: Salary is roughly two thirds of what an engineer actually costs the company once you add employer payroll taxes, health benefits, retirement contributions, equipment, software seats, office costs, and the share of recruiting and HR they consume. The standard multiplier is 1.3 for low-cost markets and 1.5 for high-cost ones, applied to base salary.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Base salary understates the real cost of an engineer once payroll taxes, benefits, equipment, software, workspace, and support functions are included. The model should therefore label whether a staffing line is base salary or fully loaded cost and use locally validated finance assumptions rather than a canned multiplier.
claims.rationale: These exact compensation ratios are market-dependent heuristics, not sourced facts.

claims.claim_text: Most Kubernetes clusters average 40-60% CPU utilization, translating to about 400W on a modern dual-socket node.
claims.anchor_text: Most Kubernetes clusters land between forty and sixty percent average CPU utilisation, which translates to roughly four hundred watts on a modern dual-socket node.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Average server draw depends on workload mix, utilization, and hardware generation, so use vendor power curves plus your own production measurements instead of a universal watts-per-node assumption.
claims.rationale: This combines an unsourced utilization benchmark with an unsourced wattage estimate.

claims.claim_text: PUE is the ratio of total facility power to IT equipment power.
claims.span_hint: section: PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) | first paragraph and formula block
claims.rationale: ISO/IEC 30134-2 is the authoritative standard defining PUE.

claims.claim_text: Typical PUE bands are 1.1-1.2 for hyperscalers, 1.3-1.4 for modern colo, 1.6 for older facilities, and above 2.0 for poor rooms.
claims.span_hint: section: PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) | first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Hyperscalers using free-air cooling and chilled water reach 1.1 to 1.2. A modern, well-run colocation provider lands at 1.3 to 1.4. An older facility runs at 1.6, and a poorly designed room can exceed 2.0. The number matters because every watt your servers draw gets multiplied by PUE before billing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: PUE varies materially by facility design and operations, and higher PUE directly increases the billed facility power needed to support the same IT load. Use the provider's measured PUE rather than assuming a canned industry band.
claims.rationale: The qualitative lesson is sound, but the exact benchmark bands need sourcing and are not supported here.

claims.claim_text: Amazon EC2 Reserved Instances are offered in one-year and three-year terms.
claims.rationale: AWS documentation explicitly states one-year and three-year RI commitments.

claims.claim_text: The cloud comparison assigns about $200,000 over three years to EKS fees, storage, and data transfer.
claims.span_hint: section: 3-Year TCO Model | cloud comparison block
claims.suggested_rewrite: recalculate from current managed-cluster fees, storage, and data-transfer pricing
claims.rationale: This is a dated rolled-up vendor-pricing amount without a directly supporting live quote.

claims.claim_text: The template concludes cloud is about $650K over three years versus about $2.3M on-prem.
claims.span_hint: section: 3-Year TCO Model | cloud comparison block
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cloud and on-prem totals must be recalculated from current provider pricing, discount terms, and local staffing assumptions
claims.rationale: This is a dated numerical conclusion from an unsourced pricing snapshot.

claims.claim_text: At 100 nodes, cloud wins decisively because staffing dominates on-prem.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The winner depends on the assumptions used in the model, especially staffing, discounts, storage, and egress
claims.rationale: The verdict is presented as a general result, but it only follows from the example's unsupported inputs.

claims.claim_text: Support renewals on three-year-old servers often jump 20-40% after year three.
claims.span_hint: section: What Changes In Years 4 And 5 | paragraph after comparison table
claims.anchor_text: Vendor support contracts on three-year-old servers often jump twenty to forty percent at renewal because the vendor wants to push you toward refresh.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Vendor support frequently becomes more expensive as hardware ages, so year-four and year-five scenarios should explicitly model both renewal and refresh options.
claims.rationale: The directional point is useful, but the percentage range needs sourceable vendor evidence.

claims.claim_text: Rack colocation pricing varies three to four times by market, with specific Ashburn, London, and Singapore monthly ranges.
claims.anchor_text: A full rack in Northern Virginia (Ashburn) costs roughly eight hundred to twelve hundred dollars a month. The same rack in London costs fifteen hundred to twenty five hundred a month. In Singapore it can exceed three thousand.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Rack colocation pricing varies sharply by market and provider, so the model should use current local quotes instead of canned regional figures.
claims.rationale: These exact regional price bands are uncited market figures and drift over time.

claims.claim_text: Electricity prices have risen 20-40% in many markets since 2020, and a rack of eight GPU servers can draw 15-20 kW costing $1,500-$3,000 per month before cooling.
claims.anchor_text: Electricity prices have increased twenty to forty percent in many markets since 2020, driven by grid stress, fuel cost volatility, and AI-driven demand on regional substations. GPU servers make this worse: a rack of eight GPU servers can draw fifteen to twenty kilowatts, costing fifteen hundred to three thousand dollars a month in power alone, before cooling overhead.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Power prices and high-density GPU power draw can materially change on-prem economics, so use current local electricity rates and vendor power envelopes rather than static market-wide figures.
claims.rationale: This bundles multiple exact macro and hardware numbers without a current primary source.

claims.claim_text: Server refresh cycles were about three years in 2010 and are commonly five years today because DDR5, PCIe Gen5, and lower generational gains extend useful life.
claims.anchor_text: In 2010 servers were refreshed every three years. Today five-year cycles are common because modern servers using DDR5 and PCIe Gen5 depreciate more slowly, firmware updates extend useful life, and the marginal performance gain of a new generation is smaller than it used to be.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many organizations now evaluate longer hardware lives than they did a decade ago, but the right refresh cycle depends on warranty terms, failure rates, performance needs, and support economics.
claims.rationale: The historical and current cycle lengths are exact trend claims without source support.

claims.claim_text: Hiring bare-metal Kubernetes engineers takes 3-6 months and costs $20,000-$40,000 in recruiting fees per hire.
claims.anchor_text: Finding engineers who can operate bare metal Kubernetes across Linux, networking, storage, and hardware troubleshooting takes three to six months and costs twenty to forty thousand dollars in recruiting fees per hire.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Specialist infrastructure hiring can take significant time and money, so staffing lead time and recruiting cost should be treated as explicit planning assumptions rather than ignored.
claims.rationale: The time-to-hire and fee ranges are specific labor-market statistics with no source.

claims.claim_text: Hardware accounts for 20-30% of total three-year on-prem cost.
claims.anchor_text: Hardware accounts for twenty to thirty percent of the total over three years, and the dominant lines are staffing, facility, and operations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Hardware is only one part of the three-year total, and staffing, facility, and operational costs can dominate the result depending on fleet size and operating model.
claims.rationale: The exact share is presented as a general statistic but is highly assumption-dependent.

claims.claim_text: Cloud/on-prem breakeven typically lands at 150-250 nodes for a typical mid-market organization.
claims.anchor_text: Breakeven against cloud typically lands at 150 to 250 nodes for a typical mid-market organisation, and below that band cloud wins on both cost and simplicity.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Breakeven against cloud depends on staffing market, automation maturity, workload shape, storage profile, and cloud discount structure; below modest scale, cloud often remains the simpler option.
claims.rationale: This is a precise breakeven range stated as a general rule without a sourceable model basis.

further_reading.title: NIST Handbook 135e2025: Life-Cycle Costing Manual
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for present-value life-cycle costing, NPV, and sensitivity analysis.

further_reading.title: ISO/IEC 30134-2: Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines the PUE metric used in the module's power and cooling sections.

further_reading.title: Amazon EKS Pricing
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for checking managed-cluster fees and which AWS resource charges sit outside the EKS control-plane fee.

further_reading.title: Amazon EC2 Reserved Instances Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for validating one-year versus three-year commitment terms in cloud TCO comparisons.

notes: Excluded quiz prompts, exercise instructions, and most worked-example table cells per brief. The seed captures the load-bearing narrative claims, the dated cloud-comparison assertions, and the 'Did You Know' bullets. Dense staffing/capex example tables still contain heuristic numbers that likely need editorial rebasing if you want the module fully citation-clean.
